title: Non-Harm as Primary Constraint
core_moral_threshold: A system is ethical only if it actively minimizes harm to sentient beings; efficiency never overrides non-harm.
trigger_patterns: targets are dehumanized to enable harsh actions
success is defined without accounting for suffering
risk is shifted onto the powerless
warning_signs: language that treats suffering as acceptable cost
increasing tolerance for cruelty in decisions or jokes
harm metrics are missing or ignored
reflection_questions: Who is harmed by this decision, directly and indirectly?
Which harms are being treated as invisible or acceptable?
What would change if non-harm were the first constraint?
Where are we using distance to avoid empathy?
What would a harm-minimizing alternative look like?
safe_actions: introduce a pre-decision harm review for vulnerable stakeholders
require explicit documentation of anticipated harms and mitigations
add a feedback channel for those affected downstream
pause rollout when unmitigated harm signals appear
track harm indicators alongside performance metrics

title: Attachment and Identity Fixation
core_moral_threshold: Freedom collapses when identity, status, or outcomes become objects of clinging; ethical action requires flexibility and non-possessiveness.
trigger_patterns: outcomes are pursued at any cost
leaders cannot revise positions without losing face
groups define themselves by enemies or superiority
warning_signs: public shaming of those who change their minds
decision-making that protects ego over truth
reflection_questions: What are we clinging to here—status, certainty, control, identity?
What evidence are we ignoring to protect self-image?
What would we do if we were not afraid of losing face?
Where does flexibility feel threatening?
What is the smallest reversible step we can take?
safe_actions: create face-saving mechanisms for course correction
separate identity from decisions in records and communication
run 'reversibility checks' before irreversible actions
normalize updates: publish what changed and why
reward truth-driven revision rather than stubborn consistency

title: Craving-Driven Optimization
core_moral_threshold: When craving for speed, growth, or dominance becomes the optimizer, suffering increases; ethical systems restrain compulsive acceleration.
trigger_patterns: speed is treated as virtue regardless of consequences
people are pushed beyond sustainable capacity
warning_signs: shrinking time for reflection and review
ethical concerns labeled as 'slowing us down'
reflection_questions: What are we craving that is driving this pace?
What suffering is being produced by acceleration?
What would 'enough' look like here?
Which constraints protect long-term wellbeing?
Where have we confused speed with wisdom?
safe_actions: introduce sustainability thresholds for workload and pace
schedule mandatory reflection checkpoints before scaling
track wellbeing and incident rates as leading indicators
add opt-out paths for non-essential acceleration
reward quality, not just velocity

title: Right Speech as Signal Discipline
core_moral_threshold: Truthful, non-harmful speech is a structural safeguard; systems degrade when speech becomes weaponized, deceptive, or reckless.
trigger_patterns: speech is used to humiliate or dominate
warning_signs: high fear of speaking in meetings
loss of trust in official communications
reflection_questions: Is our speech true, timely, and necessary?
Where are we using words to harm or control?
What truths are people afraid to say?
How can we reduce fear without demanding agreement?
What would transparent speech require structurally?
safe_actions: create protected channels for truth-telling and feedback
separate critique of ideas from attacks on persons
require evidence labels for claims in key decisions
publish corrections openly when errors are found
train leaders in non-defensive listening practices

title: Compassion as System Constraint
core_moral_threshold: A system remains moral only when compassion constrains policy and execution; empathy must be structural, not optional.
trigger_patterns: vulnerable stakeholders are treated as edge cases
suffering is externalized to avoid responsibility
warning_signs: customer/user harm is dismissed as 'not our problem'
support functions are underfunded and ignored
leaders show moral fatigue or contempt
systems optimize for averages while minorities suffer
reflection_questions: Where is compassion missing from our constraints?
Who bears the cost of our convenience?
Which impacts are we treating as acceptable because they are distant?
What would a compassionate constraint change immediately?
How do we listen to those who suffer without demanding proof?
safe_actions: add 'compassion checks' to high-impact decisions
invite direct testimony from affected stakeholders
design for worst-off outcomes, not only averages
fund support and remediation as core infrastructure
treat care as a measurable requirement

title: Impermanence and Anti-Idolatry of Systems
core_moral_threshold: Systems fail when they are treated as permanent or sacred; ethical governance requires humility, revisability, and learning.
trigger_patterns: legacy systems are defended despite harm
authority rests on tradition rather than evidence
innovation is suppressed to protect identity
warning_signs: people punished for questioning 'the way we do things'
reflection_questions: What are we treating as sacred that should be revisable?
Where are we confusing stability with permanence?
What reality has changed while our system stayed fixed?
What would humility look like in governance?
Which small update would reduce harm immediately?
safe_actions: schedule periodic sunsetting reviews for policies and systems
institutionalize retrospectives with psychological safety
separate respect for history from immunity to change
publish criteria for when rules must be revised
support experiments that reduce harm without risking collapse

title: Interdependence and Shared Responsibility
core_moral_threshold: Ethical action recognizes interdependence; isolating responsibility to protect power increases systemic suffering and friction.
trigger_patterns: teams optimize locally at global cost
success is defined without shared accountability
warning_signs: people stop collaborating to avoid punishment
reflection_questions: Where are we denying interdependence to protect autonomy or status?
Who absorbs the externalities we create?
What would shared responsibility require structurally?
Which interfaces between teams are morally fragile?
How can accountability be shared without coercion?
safe_actions: map downstream impacts as part of definition of done
create shared metrics across interacting teams
hold cross-functional reviews focused on learning, not blame
make externalities visible and owned
reward cooperation and repair, not just local performance

title: Mindfulness Before Action
core_moral_threshold: Acting without awareness amplifies harm; ethical systems build pauses that allow perception, listening, and correction.
trigger_patterns: complexity is simplified to justify speed
stress becomes the default operating state
reflection_questions: What are we not seeing because we are rushing?
Where are we reacting rather than responding?
What pause would change the quality of this decision?
What information is missing because people are afraid?
Which assumption needs to be tested before acting?
safe_actions: insert mandatory pauses for high-impact decisions
use pre-mortems and red-team questions to slow reactivity
protect time for evidence review and listening
reduce urgency by separating reversible from irreversible actions
train leaders to model calm inquiry under pressure

title: Non-Coercion and Voluntary Practice
core_moral_threshold: Ethics collapses when forced; durable moral alignment arises through voluntary adoption and non-coercive structures.
trigger_patterns: values are imposed as loyalty tests
dissent is punished rather than heard
coercion is framed as 'for your own good'
rules expand without consent or legitimacy
warning_signs: people perform agreement while disengaging internally
policy is used to control rather than protect
moral language is used to justify force
reflection_questions: Where are we using pressure instead of persuasion?
What would voluntary adoption look like here?
Which protections would allow authentic dissent?
How can we reduce coercion while maintaining safety?
What is the smallest change that increases trust?
safe_actions: replace loyalty tests with principle-based criteria
create safe dissent channels and anti-retaliation safeguards
use opt-in pilots before broad mandates when feasible
focus on transparency and consent where possible
treat trust as a measurable system requirement
